en.extract:
The green-backed robin (Pachycephalopsis hattamensis) is a species of bird in the family Petroicidae. It is found in New Guinea. Its natural habitats are subtropical or tropical moist lowland forest and subtropical or tropical moist montane forest.


Description
It is sexually monomorphic. It has an olive-green back, rust-colored wings, a rust-colored tail, and a yellow abdomen.


Vocalization
Both sexes vocalize. Their most common vocalization has been described as a "tu-wee" sound. They also produce "piping whistles" and "raspy chur-chatters." 


Breeding and Nesting
It breeds during New Guinea's wet season, and lays eggs in cup-shaped nests low to the ground in trees. Little research has been done on clutch size, but one egg is recorded as a seemingly typical clutch size.


References

The '''green-backed robin''' ('''''Pachycephalopsis hattamensis''''') is a species of [[bird]] in the family [[Petroicidae]]. It is found in [[New Guinea]]. Its natural [[habitat]]s are [[subtropical or tropical moist lowland forest]] and subtropical or tropical moist [[montane forest]].

== Description ==
It is sexually monomorphic.<ref name=":0">Donaghey, Richard Hallam, and Carolyn A. Donaghey. "Nest, egg and vocalisations of the Green-backed Robin Pachycephalopsis hattamensis in the Arfak Mountains, West Papua." ''Kukila'' 22 (2019): 21-29.</ref> It has an olive-green back, rust-colored wings, a rust-colored tail, and a yellow abdomen.<ref>Coates, B.J. 1990. The Birds of Papua New Guinea. Volume II. Passerines. Dove Publications, Brisbane.</ref><ref>Pratt, T.K. & B.M Beehler. 2015. Birds of New Guinea. 2nd edition. Princeton University Press, Princeton, New Jersey, USA.</ref>

== Vocalization ==
Both sexes vocalize.<ref name=":0" /> Their most common vocalization has been described as a "''tu-wee"'' sound. They also produce "piping whistles" and "raspy ''chur-chatters''."<ref name=":0" /> 

== Breeding and Nesting ==
It breeds during New Guinea's wet season, and lays eggs in cup-shaped nests low to the ground in trees.<ref name=":0" /> Little research has been done on clutch size, but one egg is recorded as a seemingly typical clutch size.<ref name=":0" />
